summary: Gruber discusses European Commission chief Margrethe Vestager's meeting with Tim Cook ahead of the DMA compliance deadline, predicting that nothing substantive was discussed. He speculates extensively about how Apple will implement sideloading and alternative payment requirements, expecting Apple to comply with the letter of the DMA while making sideloading difficult and unappealing for most users. He draws a parallel to GDPR, which was intended to improve European web privacy but instead produced an annoying cookie consent popup regime. Gruber predicts the DMA will similarly backfire, making the mobile experience worse in Europe rather than better.
key_points: Apple's DMA compliance plans remain entirely unknown outside Cupertino, with the March 7 deadline approaching
Gruber expects sideloading will be off by default, region-locked to the EU, and still require Apple signing — designed so very few users enable it
Apple will likely demand 27% commission from apps implementing their own payment processing, mirroring the Dutch dating app precedent
Complying with the letter of the DMA does not mean capitulating to the spirit of the DMA — Apple will steer its platform as it sees fit
The GDPR precedent suggests EU tech regulation often produces worse user experiences rather than the intended improvements
money_quotes: There are so many meetings and negotiations where I'd pay a veritable fortune to have been a fly on the wall. Vestager meeting with Cook this week was not one.
Complying with the letter of the DMA does not mean capitulating to the spirit of the DMA.

The idea that Apple is going to roll over and give up on steering their own platform the way they feel best benefits both their own bottom line, and their users' experience/privacy/security, is naive.

The European Commission expected that the GDPR would result in websites prioritizing the privacy of E.U. users — a better web in Europe than elsewhere. Instead, the result was increased user annoyance under a nonstop daily barrage of consent popovers — a worse web in Europe than elsewhere.
I suspect the same will prove true of the DMA and mobile platforms. Fireworks are coming, but it seems like few people know it.
I expect that Apple has designed sideloading in such a way that very few users will enable it.
key_insight: Apple will comply with the DMA's letter while undermining its spirit, and like GDPR before it, the regulation will likely make things worse for European users rather than better.
